summary: Jeff Atwood revisits Steve McConnell's list of classic software development mistakes, using the Gilligan's Island metaphor to illustrate how projects repeatedly fall into the same traps without escaping. He presents a case study showing how developers claim to be '99% done' while hiding significant remaining work, exemplifying the common self-deception in project estimates. The post catalogs 36 classic mistakes across people, process, product, and technology categories, along with newer additions like excessive multi-tasking and confusing estimates with targets. Atwood argues that experienced developers differ from inexperienced ones primarily in their ability to recognize when they're making mistakes, and that actively scanning for these classic errors is essential to project success.
key_points: The '99% done' phenomenon hides substantial remaining work, as the last 10% often takes as long as the first 90%
Classic mistakes are 'classic' because they're seductive - you must actively recognize when you're falling into them
Success in software projects depends less on avoiding a few wrong things than on doing almost everything right
The difference between experienced and inexperienced developers is recognizing mistakes as they happen
Survey data shows overly optimistic schedules (77%) and unrealistic expectations (73%) are the most common mistakes
money_quotes: The first ninety percent of the task takes ninety percent of the time, and the last ten percent takes the other ninety percent.
Every week there's some new, crazy scheme to escape the island, but at the end of the episode, the castaways always end up stuck on the island for yet another week.
Classic mistakes are classic because they're so seductive.
Actually succeeding in a software project depends a whole lot less on not doing a few things wrong but on doing almost everything right.
I've increasingly come to believe the only difference between experienced and inexperienced software developers is that the experienced ones realize when they're making mistakes.

If you're not actively scanning through the list of Classic Software Development Mistakes as you run your software project, you have no idea how likely it is you're making one of these mistakes right now.
Making mistakes is inevitable, but repeating the same ones over and over doesn't have to be. You should endeavor to make all-new, spectacular, never-seen-before mistakes.
key_insight: The key to escaping perpetual project failure isn't avoiding all mistakes, but developing the self-awareness to recognize classic traps as you're falling into them.
